statement.summary: This study explores participation in green energy transitions amid climate change, focusing on organizational challenges, public trust, and renewable energy adoption. It examines how California’s Public Safety Power Shutoffs (PSPS) influence household trust in utilities and drive interest in solar energy, battery storage, and self-sufficiency. Findings reveal that distrust in utilities’ intent and competence fosters greater interest in renewable technologies. Additionally, it investigates the role of Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) in rebuilding trust in utility companies, highlighting how CSR alignment with core functions influences public perceptions and participation. Lastly, it analyses renewable energy adoption in energy cooperatives, identifying demographic, economic, and cultu

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom describes empirical social science research on energy transition participation. It is provisionally validated through stated findings but remains preprint/unvalidated in peer review at claimed 2026 date (future). The causal claims (PSPS → distrust → solar interest; CSR alignment → trust rebuilding) are testable but not yet established as universal theorems. The abstract is incomplete (cuts mid-sentence), suggesting work-in-progress status.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 不信が深いほど、自律への欲求は強まり、信頼再構築には企業責任の継続的な行為が必須である。
